full_transcript:
SPEAKER_01: "So we've established that there's this strong connection between emotions and memories."
SPEAKER_01: "Now, quite a bit of research has gone into investigating the connection."
SPEAKER_01: "One study, conducted by a research team at Harvard, looked at how emotions affect how much we remember, how many details of a memory we're able to remember."
SPEAKER_01: "In the study, volunteers were shown a series of pictures."
SPEAKER_01: "Some were positive images, like a child smiling, some were negative, and some were just neutral."
SPEAKER_01: "Can anyone guess which images were the easiest to remember?"
SPEAKER_00: "The most positive and negative images?"
SPEAKER_00: "The ones that triggered the strongest emotional responses?"
SPEAKER_01: "Right."
SPEAKER_01: "Those images that generated a more extreme emotional response, positive or negative, they were easier to recall than the neutral images."
SPEAKER_01: "Pretty intuitive, right?"
SPEAKER_01: "But the scientists also found that people remember more details from a negative image than they do from a positive one."
SPEAKER_01: "How come?"
SPEAKER_01: "Well, we think it's rooted in how we respond to highly stressful or dangerous situations."
SPEAKER_01: "By remembering a negative event in detail, well, those details might be really helpful if some day you're faced with a similar situation."
SPEAKER_00: "I think I know what you mean."
SPEAKER_00: "When I was younger I was climbing a ladder and one of the rungs was wet and I nearly slipped and fell."

SPEAKER_00: "I remember just how scared I felt and I can still see the little beads of water on that step."
SPEAKER_01: "And I bet you're careful around matters to this day, aren't you?"
SPEAKER_01: "Now, another question that researchers have focused on is, are there specific areas of the brain associated with different emotional states?"
SPEAKER_01: "One neuroscientist, Antonio Demasio, tested this idea."
SPEAKER_01: "Demasio hypothesized that different emotions would trigger activity in different parts of the brain."
SPEAKER_01: "To test his hypothesis, a team of researchers hooked up some volunteers to brain scanning equipment and asked them to recall, to relive, actually, some intense memories, like the elation they felt on graduation day."
SPEAKER_01: "and the results supported Demasio's hypothesis."
SPEAKER_01: "Remembering a happy event did indeed activate one area of the brain while remembering a sad event activated another."
SPEAKER_01: "This finding that emotions of a specific type are associated with a specific brain region may help explain why, when you're feeling a certain way, the brain will activate memories of past situations associated with that particular feeling."
SPEAKER_01: "A phenomenon we call mood congruity."
SPEAKER_01: "Congruity means something like harmony between two things."
SPEAKER_01: "So mood congruity works like this."
SPEAKER_01: "If you're feeling happy, it's more likely that your brain will call up pleasant memories, like that wonderful beach vacation you took last year, which of course just reinforces your happy mood."
SPEAKER_01: "The brain is wired to support your mood."
SPEAKER_01: "It's not going to try to balance or temper it by producing memories that aren't consistent with the way you're feeling at that moment."
SPEAKER_01: "But going back to Demasio's team's experiment, there was one result that was unexpected."
SPEAKER_01: "And they discovered it when they looked at the scans of one particular region, the volunteer's prefrontal cortices."
SPEAKER_01: "Okay, who can tell me what they know about the prefrontal cortex?"

SPEAKER_00: "That's the very front of the brain."
SPEAKER_00: "It's responsible for, it's associated with generating ideas, right?"
SPEAKER_00: "With our capacity for creative thought."
SPEAKER_01: "Yes, among other things."
SPEAKER_01: "Well, what the researchers learned from the volunteer's brain scans was that when you're happy, your prefrontal cortex is extremely active."
SPEAKER_01: "But if you're depressed, your prefrontal cortex slows down."
SPEAKER_01: "It doesn't generate as many thoughts as it normally would."
SPEAKER_01: "So there's something about being sad, some change in the way the brain functions that diminishes the output from the prefrontal cortex."
SPEAKER_01: "Now, that sounds like bad news, doesn't it?"
SPEAKER_01: "Except, well, this writer, Stephen Johnson, who writes popular books about the brain, has an interesting insight about this."
SPEAKER_01: "He said that he was actually relieved to read Demasio's unexpected finding."
SPEAKER_01: "You see, Johnson says that whenever he felt sad, he stopped having interesting thoughts and ideas."
SPEAKER_01: "He didn't feel creative at all."
SPEAKER_01: "His lack of productivity made him feel even worse, which in turn, of course, prolonged his bad mood."
SPEAKER_01: "Demasio's finding made Johnson realize that his prefrontal cortex was just responding temporarily to his negative state of mind."
SPEAKER_01: "So now he just reminds himself that his creativity will return once his mood improves."

Transition sentence: Okay, who can tell me what they know about the prefrontal cortex?

full_transcript:
SPEAKER_02: "We're going to talk today about a couple of experiments related to learning in infants or babies."
SPEAKER_02: "Those areas are memory and imitation."
SPEAKER_02: "Memory and imitation, so you read Chapter 8 of your textbook, right?"
SPEAKER_02: "It deals with these topics."
SPEAKER_02: "So I'll have some questions for you."
SPEAKER_02: "The two studies I want to look at, they've been controversial."
SPEAKER_02: "That's because they show these things happening before or earlier than we used to think."
SPEAKER_02: "So first, let's look at an experiment by Caroline Roeve Collier."
SPEAKER_02: "She published an important study that looked at the cognitive abilities of infants."
SPEAKER_02: "What can you tell me about Roeve Collier's study?"
SPEAKER_01: "Um, she had a baby in a crib and above the baby, there was a toy, a mobile hanging above the crib and a ribbon was tied from the mobile to the baby's leg."
SPEAKER_01: "Um, so when the baby kicked, the mobile moved."
SPEAKER_01: "There's a picture in your book."
SPEAKER_01: "So, um, yeah."
SPEAKER_01: "When they returned the baby to the crib weeks later, the baby kicked its leg when it saw the mobile again."
SPEAKER_01: "Its foot wasn't tied to the mobile, but it was still kicking."
SPEAKER_00: "But Roe v. Collier also went on to say that was proof, evidence, uh, that the baby had remembered something, had a memory."
SPEAKER_00: "The baby had kept that information that kicking moves the mobile."
SPEAKER_02: "Good."
SPEAKER_02: "In those experiments, she typically had a two-and-a-half-month-old infant."
SPEAKER_02: "The baby kicks and gets to see the mobile move."
SPEAKER_02: "So Roevee Collier concludes that very young infants can remember retain information like this."

SPEAKER_02: "So what did critics say about her conclusions?"
SPEAKER_02: "Sarah?"
SPEAKER_00: "That it wasn't true."
SPEAKER_00: "It wasn't real memory."
SPEAKER_02: "Yes."
SPEAKER_02: "Good."
SPEAKER_02: "Here's a distinction one critic raised."
SPEAKER_02: "This researcher, a leading expert in the field, said that the baby kicking after seeing the mobile is an example of implicit memory."
SPEAKER_02: "Implicit memory refers to remembering information, but not being consciously aware of remembering."
SPEAKER_02: "For example, many motor skills."
SPEAKER_02: "Remember those are skills involving movement of the body, that kind of thing?"
SPEAKER_02: "Motor skills are forms of implicit memory, like knowing how to ride a bike."
SPEAKER_02: "So the baby's kicking has to do with implicit memory because it involves perception and motor skills."
SPEAKER_02: "The baby kicks and sees a mobile move."
SPEAKER_02: "Now, explicit memory on the other hand is, well, you tell me."
SPEAKER_02: "What is it?"
SPEAKER_00: "That's where you can remember something."
SPEAKER_00: "Remember it consciously."
SPEAKER_00: "You can remember what happened in the past."
SPEAKER_00: "That's what's usually meant by the word memory."
SPEAKER_00: "I mean, real memory."
SPEAKER_02: "OK. And most researchers, they would say that ability in the area of explicit memory doesn't happen until a baby is six months to a year old, later than what Roe v. Collier said."
SPEAKER_02: "So that distinction between two different kinds of memory is important in this debate about memory and infants."

SPEAKER_02: "And then there's another thing, another cognitive ability they tested besides memory."
SPEAKER_02: "They tested imitation."
SPEAKER_02: "That's like when a parent smiles and the baby smiles back, or the parent opens their mouth, then the baby opens its mouth."
SPEAKER_02: "Here the researcher was Andrew Meltzoff."
SPEAKER_02: "Meltzolf has done studies with babies in the first few days after they were born."
SPEAKER_02: "What did he find in his experiments?"
SPEAKER_01: "Yeah, he would do things like stick out his tongue and open his mouth and the baby was able to imitate it."
SPEAKER_01: "He thought imitation was biologically based, I think."
SPEAKER_01: "And that's because, wasn't it because they were imitating something right after they'd seen it for the first time?"
SPEAKER_02: "Yes, that's right."
SPEAKER_02: "Meltzoff showed that in the first three days of life, the babies could do a full imitative response."
SPEAKER_02: "And he thinks it's biologically based because this occurs before they've had the opportunity to observe other people engage in these behaviors, like sticking out the tongue."
SPEAKER_02: "Here again other experts disagree."
SPEAKER_02: "They say that this was just an automatic response on the baby's part."
SPEAKER_02: "Not true imitation."
SPEAKER_02: "And true imitation doesn't happen until much later, they think."
SPEAKER_02: "Meltsoff also tested something called deferred imitation."
SPEAKER_02: "It's an imitation that occurs after a time delay of hours or even days."
SPEAKER_02: "Meltsoff showed that nine-month-old infants could imitate actions that they'd seen performed 24 hours earlier, like pushing a button in a box that produced a beeping sound."
SPEAKER_02: "But some experts, like Piaget, believed that deferred imitation doesn't occur until about 18 months of age."
SPEAKER_02: "So you can see that when some of these things begin, remembering imitation isn't entirely certain yet."
SPEAKER_02: "But still, I do think psychologists will eventually find agreement on these issues."

Transition sentence: So that distinction between two different kinds of memory is important in this debate about memory and infants.

full_transcript:
SPEAKER_00: "Before we leave the topic of animal navigation, let's look at one more species and how it finds its way through its environment."
SPEAKER_00: "The species we'll look at is the blind mole rat."
SPEAKER_00: "Blind mole rats are interesting in terms of navigation because they live entirely in the dark, in elaborate underground tunnel systems."
SPEAKER_00: "So how do blind mole rats find their way around in these complicated tunnels?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, for some time we've known that blind mole rats use some combination of two different navigation systems."
SPEAKER_00: "One system relies on their sense of time and their ability to remember underground landmarks."
SPEAKER_00: "For example, let's say a mole rat wants to find its way through its tunnel system back to its nest where it sleeps."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, it goes along, then feels some hard stones or a tree root under its feet, and it basically says to itself, okay, here's where I took a left."
SPEAKER_00: "And then it might remember how long it took to get to the next turn, and so forth."
SPEAKER_00: "The mole rat goes straight or turns based on what it remembers having sensed along the way on previous trips and the time it took between turns."
SPEAKER_00: "Now the other navigation system for the mole rat relies on Earth's magnetic field."
SPEAKER_00: "Mole rats have the ability to sense the magnetic field and use it to orient themselves directionally."
SPEAKER_00: "But until recently scientists weren't sure about the role of these two different systems."

SPEAKER_00: "Recently a team of researchers conducted an experiment to answer that question."
SPEAKER_00: "Do these two navigation systems play different roles?"
SPEAKER_00: "What they did was they designed a special structure that very closely resembles a blind mole rat's tunnel system, which looks like a bicycle wheel, a central hub with multiple spokes radiating outward."
SPEAKER_00: "Now this turned out to be quite important."
SPEAKER_00: "Earlier experiments had used a more generic habitat, basically a wide open circle, which was originally designed to test magnetic navigation in other animals."
SPEAKER_00: "That design led to inconclusive results with mole rats."
SPEAKER_00: "Now with their bicycle wheel tunnel system, the researchers were able to vary the distance that the mole rats traveled between their nest and their food source by creating long routes and short routes."
SPEAKER_00: "In each trial, the mole rats started out in their nest, went to the food source, and then had to find their way back home, back to the nest."
SPEAKER_00: "To determine which navigation system they were using, the researchers tested the animals under two different conditions."
SPEAKER_00: "First, the mole rats were tested under conditions of normal magnetism, for both the trip to the food and the trip home, and under these normal conditions they all followed their original route back home, regardless of the length of the trip to the food source."
SPEAKER_00: "Then, in the second part of the experiment, the magnetic field was altered, but only for the trip home."
SPEAKER_00: "A specially constructed set of magnets was used to shift the magnetic field around the habitat 90 degrees eastward."
SPEAKER_00: "The researchers wanted to see how the mole rats responded to this shift."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, it turns out that the magnetic shift had no impact on the return route of the blind mole rats after a short trip."
SPEAKER_00: "They returned to the point where they had started from."

SPEAKER_00: "But after a longer trip, they took a totally different route, one that led them 90 degrees east of the nest."
SPEAKER_00: "Essentially, they got lost."
SPEAKER_00: "This was strong evidence that blind mole rats use magnetic navigation only for longer trips."
SPEAKER_00: "So why two navigation systems, one for long trips and one for short trips?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, for now we can only speculate, but we think that when mole rats rely on the first system using underground landmarks or their sense of time, well, they make small mistakes here and there."
SPEAKER_00: "On a short trip, this doesn't matter much."
SPEAKER_00: "The trip is short, so they can't make many mistakes."
SPEAKER_00: "But on a long trip, well, there are lots of opportunities to make small mistakes."
SPEAKER_00: "And small mistakes can add up, leaving the mole-rat far from where it wants to be."
SPEAKER_00: "So on a long trip, a magnetic navigation system is better, more useful, since Earth's magnetic field is stable, a constant, a more reliable indicator of direction."

Transition sentence: They returned to the point where they had started from.

SPEAKER_00: "Now the grasslands on some of the islands have disappeared and what we have there now is tundra, primarily low scrubby vegetation."

SPEAKER_01: "Wouldn't all the islands have that?"

SPEAKER_01: "And you're saying that changed the entire ecosystem?"
SPEAKER_00: "Oh yes, and drastically."
SPEAKER_00: "Introducing a single species can gradually transform an entire ecosystem."
SPEAKER_00: "And a study that was done on the Illusion Island shows one way this can happen."
SPEAKER_00: "So, alright."

SPEAKER_00: "That's a direct impact, right?"
SPEAKER_00: "The foxes preyed on the seabirds and they started to disappear."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, there were indirect effects too."
SPEAKER_00: "Changes in the soil and vegetation."
SPEAKER_00: "And these effects weren't obvious for a long time."
SPEAKER_00: "They were much harder to detect."
SPEAKER_00: "So, this study."
SPEAKER_00: "A biologist led a team of researchers to the Aleutian Islands, where they noticed significant differences in the islands that had foxes and the islands that didn't, especially in the vegetation."
SPEAKER_00: "It was so thick on the islands without foxes that they could barely get through it."
SPEAKER_00: "But on the islands with foxes, it was only up to their ankles."
SPEAKER_00: "And the biologist had a theory about this."
SPEAKER_00: "Can anyone guess what it might be?"
SPEAKER_01: "So the foxes are eating the seabirds."
SPEAKER_01: "Maybe the seabirds were using up the grass, like for nesting materials or something."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, think about what that would mean."
SPEAKER_01: "Oh, right."
SPEAKER_00: "What the biologist thought was, he thought it might have something to do with the soil nutrients."
SPEAKER_00: "So these researchers, to investigate this theory, they analyzed the soil on these islands."
SPEAKER_00: "They wanted to determine the nutrient content to see if it played some kind of role in transforming the vegetation from grassland to tundra."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, what they found was, I think, very interesting."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, first, the soil on the Aleutian Islands is volcanic soil, nutrient-poor soil that formed from volcanic rock."

SPEAKER_00: "And what the researchers learned was that the islands without foxes were receiving regular doses of fertilizer from the droppings of the seabirds the foxes preyed on."
SPEAKER_00: "These seabirds eat fish, and they were bringing ocean-derived nutrients to the islands in the form of droppings when they came there to nest."
SPEAKER_00: "Now the islands with foxes weren't getting this fertilizer because the birds were disappearing."
SPEAKER_00: "So now we see the indirect effects of the foxes, right?"
SPEAKER_00: "The foxes, because of their impact on the seabirds, deprived the soil of fertilizer which caused the grasslands to disappear and be replaced by tundra."
SPEAKER_00: "Tundra vegetation does well in nutrient poor soil."
SPEAKER_00: "Now that's a pretty huge impact and one that no one predicted."
SPEAKER_00: "So what can we do about it?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, we want to bring back the seabirds and we're doing that."
SPEAKER_00: "We're removing the foxes and we've been successful."
SPEAKER_00: "Most of the foxes are off the islands now and the seabirds are starting to come back."
SPEAKER_00: "But seabirds, most seabirds that is, have only one offspring a year."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, we've learned that there are now only half the number of breeding seabirds on the islands that had the foxes as there are on the islands that didn't."
SPEAKER_00: "And there were major changes in the vegetation, and the foxes were there for a long time."
SPEAKER_00: "So, does this mean that non-native species should never be introduced into an ecosystem?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, not necessarily."
SPEAKER_00: "Scientists introduce species in many different places around the world and for a number of reasons."
SPEAKER_00: "We'll talk more about that over the course of the semester."
SPEAKER_00: "It does point out the dangers of unforeseen consequences though, and it certainly suggests the need for careful planning and for further studies of the effects in habitats such as these where new species have already been introduced."

full_transcript:
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, today I want to talk about sleep."
SPEAKER_00: "We all sleep."
SPEAKER_00: "Humans sleep."
SPEAKER_00: "Many animals sleep."
SPEAKER_00: "When we sleep, we aren't actually unconscious, but in a state of reduced awareness of our surroundings."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, what does that mean for animals in the wild if they're in a state like that, unable to monitor their environment?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, they're helpless, vulnerable to predators, maybe."
SPEAKER_00: "Right."
SPEAKER_00: "They're vulnerable to predators, yet they sleep."
SPEAKER_00: "So let's talk about why, the biology of sleep."
SPEAKER_00: "So the first thing I want you to understand is that sleep's a very risky behavior."
SPEAKER_00: "And risky behaviors don't evolve unless they bring the animal some kind of benefit that outweighs that risk."
SPEAKER_00: "So sleep must have a really important function."
SPEAKER_00: "And I want to emphasize that there's a difference between sleep and other forms of inactivity, like regular rest."
SPEAKER_00: "Insects, for instance, rest, but they don't sleep."
SPEAKER_00: "And what's specific to sleep is that the brain alters many of its usual activities, including its connection to sensory and motor organs."
SPEAKER_00: "It shuts those connections down."
SPEAKER_00: "So a sleeping animal can usually neither sense nor move."
SPEAKER_00: "And I say usually because, well, we'll get to that in a minute."
SPEAKER_00: "So basically, all mammals and birds sleep."
SPEAKER_00: "But there are some unusual ways of sleeping."
SPEAKER_00: "take marine mammals, like dolphins."
SPEAKER_00: "Dolphins need to swim up for air once in a while, so they can't completely shut off all movement and sensation."
SPEAKER_00: "So their brain can't shut down completely."
SPEAKER_00: "But dolphins get around this."

SPEAKER_00: "How?"
SPEAKER_00: "they sleep one brain hemisphere at a time."
SPEAKER_00: "Sleeping dolphins actually look like they're just resting awake and occasionally swim up for air."
SPEAKER_00: "So how can we even tell they're sleeping?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, we measured their brain activity, which showed that one half was active while the other was sleeping."
SPEAKER_00: "That's some adaptation, huh?"
SPEAKER_00: "So what is the function of sleep?"
SPEAKER_00: "As I said, it must be important, but why?"
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, I know you're all tempted to say that sleeps when the whole body rests, that sleep is our mechanism to recover from physical activity."
SPEAKER_00: "And it's true, when you sleep, your muscles lose their tone, they relax, the body saves energy."
SPEAKER_00: "But this could happen even during regular waking rest."
SPEAKER_00: "I mean, that's probably what happens when insects rest."
SPEAKER_00: "You don't need sleep for that."
SPEAKER_00: "You could get the same benefit by resting awake."
SPEAKER_00: "So this doesn't explain why sleep evolved."
SPEAKER_00: "To explain the real function, the most important benefit of sleep, you'll need to focus on the brain, not the muscles."
SPEAKER_00: "Remember how we can tell that a dolphin is sleeping, not just resting?"
SPEAKER_00: "It's because of a unique pattern of brain activity, which is a clue that sleep's function has to do specifically with the brain."
SPEAKER_00: "And that's the general consensus, because that's what could explain why it evolved."
SPEAKER_00: "It also explains why sleep is a characteristic of mammals and birds, because they have the most complex brains of all animals."
SPEAKER_00: "The simpler brain, like a reptile, gets by with little sleep, or even without sleep."
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, so we know that sleep benefits the brain, but in what way we're not sure yet."
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, so one hypothesis is that during sleep, the brain synthesizes molecules that it needs for proper functioning when awake."
SPEAKER_00: "The longer we stay awake, the more those molecules get depleted."

SPEAKER_00: "So the brain needs to replenish them."
SPEAKER_00: "And this supposedly happens during sleep."
SPEAKER_00: "I'm talking about energy sources like glycogen and some types of neurotransmitters that are needed to transmit signals between nerve cells."
SPEAKER_00: "So, these get replenished during sleep, and the next morning, our brain is working with a full supply of energy and neurotransmitters again."
SPEAKER_00: "That explains why we feel so alert and mentally refreshed after night's sleep."
SPEAKER_00: "Yes, Jim?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, you know, I don't."
SPEAKER_00: "I don't work well in the morning at all."
SPEAKER_00: "Yeah, I do my best work at night too."
SPEAKER_00: "Not everyone's at their best in the morning."
SPEAKER_00: "I'll grant you that."
SPEAKER_00: "But that doesn't rule out the replenishment hypothesis."
SPEAKER_00: "It's just a bit more complicated than I said."
SPEAKER_00: "We're fairly sure that there's this other rhythm of mental activity going on that's independent of sleep."
SPEAKER_00: "It's like a pre-programmed 24-hour cycle."
SPEAKER_00: "Your mental activity peaks at a certain hour every day, like at night, for you to."
SPEAKER_00: "And then it goes down at some other point."
SPEAKER_00: "But the point is that after a sleepless night, that peak is not as high as it would normally be."
SPEAKER_00: "And the longer you go without, the lower those peaks get."
SPEAKER_00: "So the replenishment may be needed to keep the metal peaks high."
SPEAKER_00: "Does that make sense?"
SPEAKER_00: "The two mechanisms are not mutually exclusive."
SPEAKER_00: "That's what I'm trying to say."

Transition sentence: The longer we stay awake, the more those molecules get depleted.

full_transcript:
SPEAKER_01: "Okay, back in the 1930s, a biologist named G.F. Gauza first proposed what's known as Gauza's hypothesis."
SPEAKER_01: "Gauss has said that whenever you've got two similar species competing for the exact same limited resources, one of them will have some sort of advantage, however slight, that'll eventually enable this species to dominate and ultimately exclude the other one, even cause it to become extinct."
SPEAKER_01: "That's why Gauss' hypothesis came to be called the competitive exclusion principle."
SPEAKER_01: "Gauza did some lab experiments, like placing two paramecium species in the same environment where they'd have to compete for the same food."
SPEAKER_01: "He found that over time, one species was consistently able to drive out the other, to eliminate it from the habitat, just as his hypothesis predicted."
SPEAKER_01: "Now, one of the early criticisms of Gauss' hypothesis was that, sure, it works in simple lab experiments where you have just two competing species in a controlled environment, but the hypothesis falls apart when applied to natural ecosystems where things are more complex."
SPEAKER_01: "Now, it's true that in the real world there are lots of examples that seem to contradict the hypothesis."
SPEAKER_01: "For example, in the forests of New England, in the northeastern United States, there are some small songbirds called warblers."
SPEAKER_01: "And right in the same area, you've got five species of warbler, all about the same size and all having similar diets of insects, insects that are found on and around trees."
SPEAKER_01: "Yet these five warbler species all managed to coexist."
SPEAKER_01: "There's no dominance, no exclusion of one species by another."
SPEAKER_01: "How's this possible?"
SPEAKER_01: "Well, turns out that one warbler species feeds in the uppermost branches, while others favor the middle branches and others feed toward the bottom of the tree."
SPEAKER_01: "Also, each warbler species breeds at a different time of year."
SPEAKER_01: "This way, the period of peak food requirement when the birds are feeding their chicks varies from one species to the next."
SPEAKER_00: "Yes, Mark."
SPEAKER_00: "But does that really contradict Gauss' hypothesis?"

SPEAKER_00: "Because, I mean, are those different warbler species really competing for the same food?"
SPEAKER_00: "I don't think so."
SPEAKER_00: "I think they're more like, you know, almost cooperating so they don't have to compete."
SPEAKER_00: "Excellent."
SPEAKER_01: "To the casual observer, the warblers do seem to contradict Gauss' hypothesis, since they all live in the same place and eat the same types of insects."
SPEAKER_01: "But if you observe these birds more closely, the warbler species are not really competing with one another for the exact same food at the exact same time."
SPEAKER_01: "Which brings us to a really important concept in ecology."
SPEAKER_01: "The niche."
SPEAKER_01: "Mark, can you tell us what an ecological niche is?"
SPEAKER_00: "The place where the plant or animal lives."
SPEAKER_00: "You know, it's habitat."
SPEAKER_00: "For example?"
SPEAKER_00: "Like the polar bear living in the Arctic on the ice sheet."
SPEAKER_00: "The Arctic is its niche, the habitat it's adapted to survive in."
SPEAKER_01: "Okay, that's what most people think of."
SPEAKER_01: "But for biologists, the concept of a niche also includes the way an organism functions in its habitat, how it interacts with other plant and animal species, with the soil, the air, the water, and so on."
SPEAKER_01: "Okay, now let's put it all together."

SPEAKER_01: "If you have two similar species competing in the same niche, what's going to happen?"
SPEAKER_01: "Susan?"
SPEAKER_01: "One will dominate the other and eventually eliminate it."
SPEAKER_01: "Okay, so what could the weaker species do to improve its chances of survival?"
SPEAKER_01: "Maybe just move to some other area, you know, away from the competitor?"
SPEAKER_01: "That's one possibility."
SPEAKER_01: "But think of the scientific definition of a niche."
SPEAKER_01: "Think about the warblers."
SPEAKER_00: "Mark?"
SPEAKER_00: "Maybe it could find some new way of functioning in its habitat, so it wouldn't have to compete with the dominant species."
SPEAKER_00: "Keep the same habitat, but not the exact same niche."
SPEAKER_01: "Yes, um, and there are many ways to do that."
SPEAKER_01: "The dominant species feeds in one part of the tree, and you feed in another."
SPEAKER_00: "If the dominant species needs lots of water, you develop the ability to survive on very little water."
SPEAKER_01: "You survive on what's left over."
SPEAKER_01: "Water, food, nesting, or breeding sites, whatever."

SPEAKER_00: "And as you know, biogeography varies from ecosystem to ecosystem."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, one type of ecosystem that we haven't discussed yet is the island environment."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, islands have always been interesting to study because their isolation creates a kind of laboratory for examining their ecosystems."
SPEAKER_00: "In the 1960s, Robert MacArthur and E. O. Wilson developed a theory they called the equilibrium theory of island biogeography."
SPEAKER_00: "And their theory states that over time islands achieve equilibrium, a balance in terms of the number of species of plants, animals, insects, etc., that they can support."
SPEAKER_00: "And they, that is MacArthur and Wilson, developed calculations to predict the point at which equilibrium is achieved."
SPEAKER_00: "and the number of species that a given island would be able to sustain."
SPEAKER_00: "According to their theory, the total number of species depends on two things, the size and location of the island."
SPEAKER_00: "For example, they predict that there will be more species on bigger islands and that there will be more species on islands that are closer to the mainland."
SPEAKER_00: "So how does this work?"
SPEAKER_00: "According to MacArthur and Wilson, there are two opposing forces constantly at work in an island environment, pushing the island toward the state of equilibrium."
SPEAKER_00: "The first force is the immigration rate."
SPEAKER_00: "The immigration rate tracks the addition of new species to an island."
SPEAKER_00: "And they argue that this rate declines over time, that there are more new species early on when the island's still empty and there's little competition for space."
SPEAKER_00: "However, as time progresses, there'll be fewer and fewer new species that can compete with established species."
SPEAKER_00: "It's not impossible for a new species to be introduced at this late stage."
SPEAKER_00: "It might adapt better to some change in the evolving habitat, but it is definitely tougher."

SPEAKER_00: "The second force is the extinction rate, and this is the rate at which species disappear from the island."
SPEAKER_00: "In contrast with the immigration rate, this is a force that increases over time."
SPEAKER_00: "In the beginning, when there's plenty of space, species don't need to compete with each other quite so much."
SPEAKER_00: "But as species get settled on the island and the amount of space for each species declines, conditions become much more competitive."
SPEAKER_00: "And it's not just the weakest species that may disappear."
SPEAKER_00: "On a crowded island, there are likely to be smaller numbers of every species, which makes them all more vulnerable to things like sudden changes in the climate or some kind of natural disaster."
SPEAKER_00: "So there are these two opposing forces."
SPEAKER_00: "And at a certain point in the island's ecological evolution, they cancel each other out."
SPEAKER_00: "Immigration matches extinction."
SPEAKER_00: "At that point, equilibrium is achieved."
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, if you accept these assumptions, then MacArthur and Wilson's predictions about size and location make sense."
SPEAKER_00: "If an island is bigger, then there's more space for a greater number of species to compete and survive."
SPEAKER_00: "And if one island is closer or easier to get to from the mainland than another,"
SPEAKER_00: "then more species will be able to immigrate to the island from the mainland and establish themselves there before a state of equilibrium occurs."
SPEAKER_00: "Now notice what this theory doesn't predict."
SPEAKER_00: "It doesn't say a thing about what type of species will be on the island."
SPEAKER_00: "It doesn't predict which new species will arrive or which existing species will disappear."
SPEAKER_00: "However, we can get some sense for what those species might be by looking at the example of Krakatoa."

SPEAKER_00: "It has a volcano that erupted in 1883."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, the destruction of the ecosystem was total."
SPEAKER_00: "Nothing survived on that island."
SPEAKER_00: "Of course, it only took a few years before the island started to support life again, but it couldn't just recreate its old ecosystem."
SPEAKER_00: "The first species to take hold were some weeds, the sort of pioneering organisms that do well after the collapse of an ecosystem."
SPEAKER_00: "Beetles and spiders also returned."
SPEAKER_00: "In time, a grassland ecosystem developed."
SPEAKER_00: "So once there was some more established plant life, birds and bats were attracted to the island."
SPEAKER_00: "And they, in turn, brought, well, dropped seeds from fruit-bearing trees."
SPEAKER_00: "As trees gained a foothold on the island, forests eventually replaced the grasslands."
SPEAKER_00: "And some pioneer species disappeared, the ones that couldn't survive in a forest environment."
SPEAKER_00: "But does Krakatoa prove the equilibrium theory?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, nothing about it disproves it so far."
SPEAKER_00: "Immigration and extinction rates have generally behaved according to the theory."
SPEAKER_00: "The number of species on the island corresponds with the author's calculations and seems to be stable."
SPEAKER_00: "It appears that the island has achieved the type of equilibrium state that the theory predicts."
SPEAKER_00: "However, some recent studies suggest that other factors may also play roles in the development of an island's biogeography."

full_transcript:
SPEAKER_00: "So, we pretty much know how the biggest stars die."
SPEAKER_00: "When a massive star reaches the end of its existence, it explodes, sending out a huge blast of energy, which then fades to invisibility within a few weeks or months."
SPEAKER_00: "When a giant star explodes like this, it's called a supernova."
SPEAKER_00: "To the naked eye, a supernova appears to be a bright new star, but it's not a new star."
SPEAKER_00: "It's a dying star."
SPEAKER_00: "However, the shock waves from a supernova excite nearby clouds of hydrogen gas, causing them to compress, which does form new stars, and that process emits a distinctive type of radiation."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, a particular interest to us today is that the same type of radiation has been observed in places where galaxies have collided."
SPEAKER_00: "And in those same areas, we've also observed a high rate of new star formation."
SPEAKER_00: "So, not surprisingly, we've had a theory that colliding galaxies caused the new star formation."
SPEAKER_00: "But there was no direct evidence, that is until recently, when some European astronomers found clouds of excited hydrogen gas that could only have been caused by shock waves from a pair of colliding galaxies."
SPEAKER_00: "You know, we used to think of galaxies as isolated systems, like islands in the sea of space."
SPEAKER_00: "Now we know that they're always moving, usually in clusters."
SPEAKER_00: "I mean, think about all the gravitational forces involved."
SPEAKER_00: "We're talking about huge systems of stars and gas and dust."
SPEAKER_00: "And since the average separation between galaxies is only about 20 times their diameter, it isn't surprising that they often meet."
SPEAKER_00: "We call it an encounter."
SPEAKER_00: "And they meet in a variety of ways."
Unknown: "Thank you."

SPEAKER_00: "Mergers, for example, they're the most extreme kinds of galaxy encounters."
SPEAKER_00: "A merger is when two galaxies meet and they don't have enough momentum to keep going on their separate ways."
SPEAKER_00: "So they merge, becoming one galaxy."
SPEAKER_00: "Usually a larger galaxy will absorb, sort of swallow a smaller one."
SPEAKER_00: "This can trigger huge areas of star formation as enormous clouds of gas from the two galaxies collide and collapse into very active star-forming regions."
SPEAKER_00: "Collisions are less violent than mergers, that is, they create fewer or relatively less active areas of star formation."
SPEAKER_00: "The galaxies pass through each other, each going its separate way after they collide."
SPEAKER_00: "The collision sends out a ripple of energy into space, plowing gas and dust in front of it."
SPEAKER_00: "Your textbook has a great picture of a galaxy collision taken by the Hubble Space Telescope."
SPEAKER_00: "See the cartwheel galaxy on the left?"
SPEAKER_00: "It looks like the ripples in a pond after a stone's been tossed in."
SPEAKER_00: "That's because one of the small galaxies on the right's passed through it."
SPEAKER_00: "and that bright ring around it, that's where colliding and compressing gas is forming stars."
SPEAKER_00: "But which galaxy collided with it?"
SPEAKER_00: "The one on the upper right's been stripped of gas and dust, which is one clear sign that there's been a collision, but the one on the bottom's showing a lot of new star formation, which is also evidence of a collision."
SPEAKER_00: "So we're just not sure."
SPEAKER_00: "Finally, we have interactions."
SPEAKER_00: "In this type of encounter, the galaxies exchange gas and dust but don't actually collide."

SPEAKER_00: "Many astronomers believe that the trail of gas and dust that stretches from our Milky Way galaxy to one nearby is the result of an interaction, when the gravitational forces of the Milky Way pull some dust and gas off of the other galaxy."
SPEAKER_00: "What about stars?"
SPEAKER_00: "Do they ever collide?"
SPEAKER_00: "Actually, almost never."
SPEAKER_00: "Stars are very small compared to the size of a galaxy, and they're pretty far apart from each other."
SPEAKER_00: "Small stars."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, that term's relative, isn't it?"
SPEAKER_00: "It's hard to imagine the massive distances we're talking about here."
SPEAKER_00: "Even encounter seems like an odd term as these events take a long time."
SPEAKER_00: "We're talking billions of years, which is why most of what we theorize about comes from computer simulations."
SPEAKER_00: "Otherwise, all we'd see is a sort of freeze frame of whatever stage the encounter is in, a single picture out of a long sequence of events."
SPEAKER_00: "But I mentioned some new evidence."
SPEAKER_00: "That's come from a pair of galaxies called the Antennae."
SPEAKER_00: "The antennae are at an early stage of encounter, what'll probably be a merger, and excited hydrogen gas has been found in the regions where the two galaxies overlap."
SPEAKER_00: "We know because that distinctive radiation signature is present, and we know that there aren't enough supernovae to explain the radiation."
SPEAKER_00: "The only other possibility is that we're seeing the result of shock waves produced by the collision of these two galaxies."

Transition sentence: In this type of encounter, the galaxies exchange gas and dust but don't actually collide.
